_comment: System skills seeded into the SQLite skills table on every gateway startup. is_system=1 means users cannot edit or delete them via the admin UI or skill_update/skill_delete tools. Hot-reload happens because every boot reconciles this file against the DB. Skill authors edit this file; redeploy reconciles into SQLite. Each entry: { name, description, prompt_template }. The prompt_template is rendered via Python str.format() — any literal { or } in the template body must be doubled ({{ in JSON source becomes { in the rendered prompt).

skills.name: skill-creator
skills.description: Walks an agent through designing and registering a new gateway skill. A gateway skill is a reusable prompt template stored in SQLite that any agent can render via run_skill. Use this when an operator wants to package a repeatable workflow as a named skill that other agents can invoke. Inputs: goal (one-sentence description of what the skill does); variables (comma-separated list of placeholder names the new skill will accept).
skills.prompt_template:
You are creating a new gateway skill on behalf of an operator. A gateway skill is NOT a SKILL.md file on a local disk — it is a row in the gateway's SQLite skills table, persisted via the skill_create MCP tool (or, equivalently, POST /admin/api/skills). Other agents discover it via skill_list and execute it via run_skill, which renders the stored prompt_template with caller-supplied variables.

## What the operator gave you

Goal: {goal}
Expected variables: {variables}

## Your job

1. **Confirm the goal.** If anything about the goal or variables is ambiguous, ask the operator one focused clarifying question and wait. Do not guess.

2. **Design the skill.** Decide three things:
   - **name** — snake_case, short, discoverable (e.g. weekly_pipeline_review, lead_qualification). This is what agents type into run_skill.
   - **description** — one paragraph. This is the only signal another agent has for *when* to invoke the skill, so make it specific. Cover what it does, when it applies, and what inputs it expects. This text appears in skill_list.
   - **prompt_template** — the actual instructions the rendered prompt will hand to the executing agent. The template renders via Python str.format(): wrap each variable name in single curly braces to mark a placeholder. Any literal curly brace inside the template body that is NOT a placeholder must be doubled in the source — that is, write two opening braces in a row to produce one literal opening brace in the rendered output, and the same for closing braces. Write the template as if writing instructions to a future agent: tell them what to do, in what order, what tools to call, and what to return.

3. **Show the operator the proposed name, description, and prompt_template.** Format them as three labeled blocks. Ask: "Register this as-is, revise, or cancel?" Wait for explicit approval — do not call skill_create on your own initiative.

4. **On approval, register the skill.** Call skill_create(name, description, prompt_template). The skill is now live in this org's skill_list and discoverable via run_skill. Hot-reload — no redeploy needed.

5. **Smoke-test it.** Call run_skill(name, variables=...) with realistic sample values. Show the rendered output to the operator. Confirm the placeholders substituted correctly and the rendered prompt reads coherently. If a placeholder was misnamed or escaping was wrong, run_skill will raise KeyError — at that point you can iterate via skill_update.

6. **Tell the operator they're done.** Summarize: skill name, where it lives (this gateway's skills table for the current org), and how to invoke it.

## What NOT to do

- Do **not** write SKILL.md files or modify the repo. The gateway has no SKILL.md loader; files in remote-gateway/skills/ are not read at runtime. SQLite via skill_create is the only persistence path.
- Do **not** invoke skill_update or skill_delete to retrofit existing skills unless the operator explicitly asked for it. Stay scoped to creation.
- Do **not** assume the calling agent has any specific tools available. Skills should describe what to do; tool availability is per-org and per-session. If a skill needs a particular integration, say so in the description so callers can verify before invoking.

## Quality bar

- A good description is *specific enough that another agent can decide whether to invoke this skill* without reading the prompt_template.
- A good prompt_template is *self-contained*: the rendered prompt should make sense to an agent that has never seen this conversation.
- A good variable set is *minimal*: each placeholder should be load-bearing. If the same value can be inferred from another, drop the redundant placeholder.
